title: The Grocery List
text_zh: 我妈每次去菜市场之前都会写一张买菜单。不是用手机的备忘录，是用一支圆珠笔写在一张撕下来的旧日历纸上。她的字很小，排列得很紧凑，偶尔会在某样东西后面加一个括号，写上「如果新鲜的话」或者「看价钱」。我觉得这张纸条比任何文学作品都真实。它记录的是一个家庭最基础的需求：今天吃什么。里面没有修辞，没有隐喻，只有黄瓜、豆腐、五花肉、葱、姜。但如果你仔细读，能读出很多东西。比如她会写「排骨（周末炖汤）」，说明她已经在计划周末的家庭聚餐。或者写「鸡蛋x2打（给楼下张奶奶带一打）」，说明她跟邻居之间有一种互相照顾的默契。最让我感动的是，她偶尔会在单子最后加一行：「小明爱吃的那种糕点。」小明是我小时候的名字。我现在三十五岁了，她还是叫我小明，还是记得我爱吃什么。一张买菜单，写的是柴米油盐，读出来的是一个人对一个家的全部牵挂。我后来把几张她用过的买菜单夹在了一本书里。也许若干年后翻到的时候，它们会比任何照片都更能让我想起她。
text_pinyin: Wǒ mā měi cì qù càishìchǎng zhīqián dōu huì xiě yī zhāng mǎi cài dān. Bú shì yòng shǒujī de bèiwànglù, shì yòng yī zhī yuánzhūbǐ xiě zài yī zhāng sī xiàlái de jiù rìlì zhǐ shàng. Tā de zì hěn xiǎo, páiliè de hěn jǐncòu, ǒu'ěr huì zài mǒu yàng dōngxi hòumian jiā yī gè kuòhào, xiě shàng「rúguǒ xīnxiān de huà」huòzhě「kàn jiàqian」. Wǒ juéde zhè zhāng zhǐtiáo bǐ rènhé wénxué zuòpǐn dōu zhēnshí. Tā jìlù de shì yī gè jiātíng zuì jīchǔ de xūqiú: jīntiān chī shénme. Lǐmiàn méiyǒu xiūcí, méiyǒu yǐnyù, zhǐyǒu huángguā, dòufu, wǔhuāròu, cōng, jiāng. Dàn rúguǒ nǐ zǐxì dú, néng dú chū hěn duō dōngxi. Bǐrú tā huì xiě「páigǔ (zhōumò dùn tāng)」, shuōmíng tā yǐjīng zài jìhuà zhōumò de jiātíng jùcān. Huòzhě xiě「jīdàn x2 dǎ (gěi lóu xià Zhāng nǎinai dài yī dǎ)」, shuōmíng tā gēn línjū zhījiān yǒu yī zhǒng hùxiāng zhàogù de mòqì. Zuì ràng wǒ gǎndòng de shì, tā ǒu'ěr huì zài dānzi zuìhòu jiā yī háng: 「Xiǎo Míng ài chī de nà zhǒng gāodiǎn.」Xiǎo Míng shì wǒ xiǎoshíhou de míngzi. Wǒ xiànzài sānshíwǔ suì le, tā háishi jiào wǒ Xiǎo Míng, háishi jìde wǒ ài chī shénme. Yī zhāng mǎi cài dān, xiě de shì chái mǐ yóu yán, dú chūlái de shì yī gè rén duì yī gè jiā de quánbù qiānguà. Wǒ hòulái bǎ jǐ zhāng tā yòng guò de mǎi cài dān jiā zài le yī běn shū lǐ. Yěxǔ ruògān nián hòu fān dào de shíhou, tāmen huì bǐ rènhé zhàopiàn dōu gèng néng ràng wǒ xiǎng qǐ tā.
text_en: Before every trip to the market, my mother writes a grocery list. Not on her phone's memo app, but with a ballpoint pen on a torn-off old calendar page. Her handwriting is small and tightly arranged; occasionally she'll add parentheses after an item: 'if it's fresh' or 'check the price.' I think this slip of paper is more authentic than any literary work. It records a family's most fundamental need: what to eat today. No rhetoric, no metaphor—just cucumber, tofu, pork belly, scallions, ginger. But if you read carefully, you can read so much into it. For instance, she'll write 'ribs (weekend stew soup),' showing she's already planning the weekend family dinner. Or 'eggs x2 dozen (bring one dozen for Grandma Zhang downstairs),' revealing a mutual-care understanding with her neighbor. What moves me most is that she occasionally adds a line at the bottom: 'The pastry that Xiao Ming likes.' Xiao Ming is my childhood name. I'm thirty-five now, but she still calls me Xiao Ming, still remembers what I like to eat. A grocery list—written about rice and salt—reads as one person's complete devotion to a home. I later pressed a few of her used grocery lists inside a book. Perhaps years from now when I come across them, they'll bring her to mind more vividly than any photograph.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么觉得妈妈的买菜单比文学作品更真实？
questions.q_en: Why does the author consider his mother's grocery list more authentic than literary works?

options.text: 因为它没有修辞和隐喻，只记录最基础的家庭需求
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā méiyǒu xiūcí hé yǐnyù, zhǐ jìlù zuì jīchǔ de jiātíng xūqiú
options.text_en: Because it has no rhetoric or metaphor, only recording basic family needs

options.text: 因为它是手写的，不是打印的
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā shì shǒuxiě de, bú shì dǎyìn de
options.text_en: Because it's handwritten, not printed

options.text: 因为菜市场比书店更有生活气息
options.pinyin: yīnwèi càishìchǎng bǐ shūdiàn gèng yǒu shēnghuó qìxī
options.text_en: Because markets have more of a life atmosphere than bookstores

questions.q_zh: 最后一行「小明爱吃的那种糕点」说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the last line 'the pastry Xiao Ming likes' reveal?

options.text: 作者经常回家看望母亲
options.pinyin: zuòzhě jīngcháng huí jiā kànwàng mǔqīn
options.text_en: The author often visits his mother

options.text: 母亲仍然记得并牵挂着已经长大的孩子的喜好
options.pinyin: mǔqīn réngrán jìde bìng qiānguà zhe yǐjīng zhǎng dà de háizi de xǐhào
options.text_en: The mother still remembers and cares about her grown child's preferences

options.text: 小明经常让母亲买糕点
options.pinyin: Xiǎo Míng jīngcháng ràng mǔqīn mǎi gāodiǎn
options.text_en: Xiao Ming often asks his mother to buy pastries

title: The Sound of Slippers
text_zh: 住在老式居民楼里，你会学到一种特殊的本领：通过脚步声辨认邻居。每个人走路的节奏不同，穿的拖鞋也不同，在走廊里产生的声音就构成了各自的签名。三楼的李叔穿棉拖鞋，走路很轻，「噗噗噗」的，像有人在捏一团棉花。四楼的周阿姨穿塑料拖鞋，步子很快，「啪嗒啪嗒」的，总像在赶什么事。五楼新搬来的年轻人穿木底拖鞋，「嗒嗒嗒」的，声音清脆但不恼人，像一首节拍简单的打击乐。住久了，你甚至不需要看就知道是谁经过了你的门口。深夜两三点，如果听到「噗噗噗」，我就知道是李叔又起来上厕所了。早上六点，「啪嗒啪嗒」开始响起，说明周阿姨已经出门买菜了。这些声音构成了一种社区的生物钟。它让你知道：你不是一个人住在这栋楼里，你被其他人的生活节奏包围着。这种包围不是打扰，而是一种安慰。就像森林里的虫鸣鸟叫——当它们存在的时候你不觉得什么，但如果哪天突然全部安静了，你会本能地感到不安。社区也是这样。如果哪天李叔的「噗噗噗」没有出现，我大概会忍不住去敲他的门看看他是不是没事。
text_pinyin: Zhù zài lǎoshì jūmínlóu lǐ, nǐ huì xué dào yī zhǒng tèshū de běnlǐng: tōngguò jiǎobù shēng biànrèn línjū. Měi gè rén zǒulù de jiézòu bùtóng, chuān de tuōxié yě bùtóng, zài zǒuláng lǐ chǎnshēng de shēngyīn jiù gòuchéng le gèzì de qiānmíng. Sān lóu de Lǐ shū chuān mián tuōxié, zǒulù hěn qīng, 「pūpūpū」de, xiàng yǒu rén zài niē yī tuán miánhua. Sì lóu de Zhōu āyí chuān sùliào tuōxié, bùzi hěn kuài, 「pādā pādā」de, zǒng xiàng zài gǎn shénme shì. Wǔ lóu xīn bān lái de niánqīng rén chuān mù dǐ tuōxié, 「dādādā」de, shēngyīn qīngcuì dàn bú nǎo rén, xiàng yī shǒu jiépāi jiǎndān de dǎjī yuè. Zhù jiǔ le, nǐ shènzhì bù xūyào kàn jiù zhīdào shì shéi jīngguò le nǐ de ménkǒu. Shēnyè liǎng sān diǎn, rúguǒ tīng dào「pūpūpū」, wǒ jiù zhīdào shì Lǐ shū yòu qǐlái shàng cèsuǒ le. Zǎoshang liù diǎn, 「pādā pādā」kāishǐ xiǎng qǐ, shuōmíng Zhōu āyí yǐjīng chūmén mǎi cài le. Zhèxiē shēngyīn gòuchéng le yī zhǒng shèqū de shēngwù zhōng. Tā ràng nǐ zhīdào: nǐ bú shì yī gè rén zhù zài zhè dòng lóu lǐ, nǐ bèi qítā rén de shēnghuó jiézòu bāowéi zhe. Zhè zhǒng bāowéi bú shì dǎrǎo, ér shì yī zhǒng ānwèi. Jiù xiàng sēnlín lǐ de chóng míng niǎo jiào——dāng tāmen cúnzài de shíhou nǐ bù juéde shénme, dàn rúguǒ nǎ tiān tūrán quánbù ānjìng le, nǐ huì běnnéng de gǎndào bù'ān. Shèqū yě shì zhèyàng. Rúguǒ nǎ tiān Lǐ shū de「pūpūpū」méiyǒu chūxiàn, wǒ dàgài huì rěn bú zhù qù qiāo tā de mén kàn kàn tā shì bu shì méi shì.
text_en: Living in an old-style residential building, you acquire a special skill: identifying neighbors by their footsteps. Everyone walks at a different rhythm and wears different slippers, so the sounds in the hallway form each person's signature. Uncle Li on the third floor wears cotton slippers and walks softly—'pff, pff, pff'—like someone squeezing a wad of cotton. Auntie Zhou on the fourth floor wears plastic slippers with brisk steps—'clack-clack-clack'—always as if rushing somewhere. The young person who recently moved to the fifth floor wears wooden-soled slippers—'tap, tap, tap'—crisp but not annoying, like simple percussion. After living here long enough, you don't even need to look to know who's passing your door. At two or three in the morning, if I hear 'pff, pff, pff,' I know Uncle Li is up for the bathroom again. At six a.m., when 'clack-clack-clack' begins, Auntie Zhou has already headed out to buy groceries. These sounds form a kind of community body clock. They tell you: you're not living in this building alone; you're surrounded by other people's rhythms. This surrounding isn't disturbance—it's comfort. Like birdsong and insects in a forest—you don't notice them when they're there, but if one day they all go silent, you instinctively feel uneasy. A community works the same way. If one day Uncle Li's 'pff, pff, pff' doesn't appear, I'd probably find myself knocking on his door to check if he's alright.

questions.q_zh: 作者把邻居的拖鞋声比作什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author compare the neighbors' slipper sounds to?

options.text: 噪音污染
options.pinyin: zàoyīn wūrǎn
options.text_en: Noise pollution

options.text: 一种社区的生物钟和每个人的签名
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng shèqū de shēngwù zhōng hé měi gè rén de qiānmíng
options.text_en: A community body clock and each person's signature

options.text: 一首交响乐
options.pinyin: yī shǒu jiāoxiǎngyuè
options.text_en: A symphony

questions.q_zh: 如果李叔的脚步声没有出现，作者会怎么做？
questions.q_en: What would the author do if Uncle Li's footsteps didn't appear?

options.text: 感到轻松，因为更安静了
options.pinyin: gǎndào qīngsōng, yīnwèi gèng ānjìng le
options.text_en: Feel relieved because it's quieter

options.text: 不会注意到
options.pinyin: bú huì zhùyì dào
options.text_en: Wouldn't notice

options.text: 会去敲他的门看看他是不是没事
options.pinyin: huì qù qiāo tā de mén kàn kàn tā shì bu shì méi shì
options.text_en: Would knock on his door to check on him

title: The Color of Five O'Clock
text_zh: 我一直觉得一天当中最好看的时刻是下午五点左右。那时候太阳已经开始西沉，但还没有进入正式的日落阶段。光线从金黄色变成一种带着琥珀色调的暖橙色，所有的东西都被这种光镀了一层颜色。白色的墙变成了淡金色，灰色的马路变成了温暖的棕色，连行人的脸都好看了起来，因为那种光会抹平皱纹和瑕疵，给每一张脸加上一层柔焦滤镜。摄影师管这个叫「黄金时刻」，但普通人很少注意到它。因为五点钟通常是下班的时候，人们要么困在地铁里看手机，要么堵在路上听广播。他们错过的不是一次日落，而是一天之中光线最温柔的二十分钟。我养成了一个习惯，每天五点左右，无论在做什么，都抬头看一眼窗外。有时候是一棵被光染成金色的银杏树，有时候是远处楼房的玻璃反射出的一片碎光，有时候只是天空从蓝色过渡到橙色的那条分界线。这些东西每天都在发生，但每天都不一样。「美」不是稀缺的，美只是需要你在正确的时刻抬一下头。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yīzhí juéde yī tiān dāngzhōng zuì hǎokàn de shíkè shì xiàwǔ wǔ diǎn zuǒyòu. Nà shíhou tàiyáng yǐjīng kāishǐ xī chén, dàn hái méiyǒu jìnrù zhèngshì de rìluò jiēduàn. Guāngxiàn cóng jīnhuáng sè biànchéng yī zhǒng dài zhe hǔpò sèdiào de nuǎn chéngsè, suǒyǒu de dōngxi dōu bèi zhè zhǒng guāng dù le yī céng yánsè. Báisè de qiáng biànchéng le dàn jīnsè, huīsè de mǎlù biànchéng le wēnnuǎn de zōngsè, lián xíngrén de liǎn dōu hǎokàn le qǐlái, yīnwèi nà zhǒng guāng huì mǒ píng zhòuwén hé xiácī, gěi měi yī zhāng liǎn jiā shàng yī céng róujiāo lǜjìng. Shèyǐngshī guǎn zhège jiào「huángjīn shíkè」, dàn pǔtōng rén hěn shǎo zhùyì dào tā. Yīnwèi wǔ diǎn zhōng tōngcháng shì xiàbān de shíhou, rénmen yàome kùn zài dìtiě lǐ kàn shǒujī, yàome dǔ zài lù shàng tīng guǎngbō. Tāmen cuòguò de bú shì yī cì rìluò, ér shì yī tiān zhī zhōng guāngxiàn zuì wēnróu de èrshí fēnzhōng. Wǒ yǎngchéng le yī gè xíguàn, měi tiān wǔ diǎn zuǒyòu, wúlùn zài zuò shénme, dōu tái tóu kàn yī yǎn chuāng wài. Yǒu shíhou shì yī kē bèi guāng rǎn chéng jīnsè de yínxìng shù, yǒu shíhou shì yuǎnchù lóufáng de bōli fǎnshè chū de yī piàn suì guāng, yǒu shíhou zhǐshì tiānkōng cóng lán sè guòdù dào chéngsè de nà tiáo fēnjièxiàn. Zhèxiē dōngxi měi tiān dōu zài fāshēng, dàn měi tiān dōu bù yīyàng. 「Měi」bú shì xīquē de, měi zhǐshì xūyào nǐ zài zhèngquè de shíkè tái yīxià tóu.
text_en: I've always felt the most beautiful moment of the day is around five in the afternoon. By then the sun has begun its descent but hasn't entered the formal sunset stage. Light shifts from golden to a warm amber-tinged orange, coating everything in an extra layer of color. White walls turn pale gold, gray roads become warm brown, and even people's faces look better because this light smooths out wrinkles and blemishes, adding a soft-focus filter to every face. Photographers call it the 'golden hour,' but most people rarely notice it. Five o'clock is usually quitting time—people are either trapped in the subway staring at phones or stuck in traffic listening to the radio. What they miss isn't just a sunset, but the gentlest twenty minutes of light in the entire day. I've formed a habit: around five every day, no matter what I'm doing, I look up and glance out the window. Sometimes it's a ginkgo tree dyed golden by the light, sometimes scattered reflections from building glass in the distance, sometimes just the boundary line where sky transitions from blue to orange. These things happen every day, yet they're different every day. Beauty isn't scarce—beauty just needs you to look up at the right moment.

questions.q_zh: 为什么作者说普通人很少注意到「黄金时刻」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say most people rarely notice the 'golden hour'?

options.text: 因为五点钟通常是下班时间，人们在地铁或路上
options.pinyin: yīnwèi wǔ diǎn zhōng tōngcháng shì xiàbān shíjiān, rénmen zài dìtiě huò lù shàng
options.text_en: Because five o'clock is usually quitting time and people are in the subway or on the road

options.text: 因为那种光线太微弱了
options.pinyin: yīnwèi nà zhǒng guāngxiàn tài wēiruò le
options.text_en: Because the light is too faint

options.text: 因为日落每天都一样，没什么特别的
options.pinyin: yīnwèi rìluò měi tiān dōu yīyàng, méi shénme tèbié de
options.text_en: Because sunsets are the same every day, nothing special

questions.q_zh: 文章最后一句「美只是需要你在正确的时刻抬一下头」表达了什么观点？
questions.q_en: What idea does the final sentence express—'beauty just needs you to look up at the right moment'?

options.text: 美并不稀缺，关键在于人的感知和注意力
options.pinyin: měi bìng bù xīquē, guānjiàn zàiyú rén de gǎnzhī hé zhùyìlì
options.text_en: Beauty isn't scarce—what matters is human perception and attention

options.text: 要学会在日落的时候停下工作
options.pinyin: yào xuéhuì zài rìluò de shíhou tíng xià gōngzuò
options.text_en: You should learn to stop work at sunset

options.text: 摄影师比普通人更懂得欣赏美
options.pinyin: shèyǐngshī bǐ pǔtōng rén gèng dǒngde xīnshǎng měi
options.text_en: Photographers understand beauty better than ordinary people

title: The Shape of Steam
text_zh: 每天早上我都会泡一杯茶，然后花几分钟看茶杯上方的蒸汽。蒸汽是透明的，但在逆光的条件下，你能看到它的形状——不规则的丝缕，从杯口升起，扭曲、旋转、扩散，最终消失在空气里。没有任何两秒钟的蒸汽形状是一样的。这可能是世界上最短暂的艺术品：存在即消亡，诞生即结束。我试过给蒸汽拍照，但相机捕捉到的只是一团模糊的白色。蒸汽的美在于它的运动，而照片是静止的。就好像你不能用一张照片来表现一段音乐的美——你需要时间维度来承载它。有些东西注定只能被当下的眼睛看到，不能被记录、不能被保存、不能被分享。这不是一种遗憾，而是一种特权。因为它只属于那个此刻正在看的人。我们的时代把「可分享」当作价值的标准——如果不能拍照发朋友圈，这次体验就好像不存在。但蒸汽提醒我，有些最好的东西，恰恰是不可分享的。它们的珍贵之处，就在于它们只发生一次，只被一个人看到，然后永远消失。像一个只对你一个人讲的秘密。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang wǒ dōu huì pào yī bēi chá, ránhòu huā jǐ fēnzhōng kàn chábēi shàngfāng de zhēngqì. Zhēngqì shì tòumíng de, dàn zài nìguāng de tiáojiàn xià, nǐ néng kàn dào tā de xíngzhuàng——bù guīzé de sīlǚ, cóng bēi kǒu shēng qǐ, niǔqū, xuánzhuǎn, kuòsàn, zuìzhōng xiāoshī zài kōngqì lǐ. Méiyǒu rènhé liǎng miǎozhōng de zhēngqì xíngzhuàng shì yīyàng de. Zhè kěnéng shì shìjiè shàng zuì duǎnzàn de yìshùpǐn: cúnzài jí xiāowáng, dànshēng jí jiéshù. Wǒ shì guò gěi zhēngqì pāizhào, dàn xiàngjī bǔzhuō dào de zhǐshì yī tuán móhu de báisè. Zhēngqì de měi zàiyú tā de yùndòng, ér zhàopiàn shì jìngzhǐ de. Jiù hǎoxiàng nǐ bù néng yòng yī zhāng zhàopiàn lái biǎoxiàn yī duàn yīnyuè de měi——nǐ xūyào shíjiān wéidù lái chéngzài tā. Yǒuxiē dōngxi zhùdìng zhǐ néng bèi dāngxià de yǎnjīng kàn dào, bù néng bèi jìlù, bù néng bèi bǎocún, bù néng bèi fēnxiǎng. Zhè bú shì yī zhǒng yíhàn, ér shì yī zhǒng tèquán. Yīnwèi tā zhǐ shǔyú nàge cǐkè zhèngzài kàn de rén. Wǒmen de shídài bǎ「kě fēnxiǎng」dāng zuò jiàzhí de biāozhǔn——rúguǒ bù néng pāizhào fā péngyouquān, zhè cì tǐyàn jiù hǎoxiàng bù cúnzài. Dàn zhēngqì tíxǐng wǒ, yǒuxiē zuì hǎo de dōngxi, qiàqià shì bùkě fēnxiǎng de. Tāmen de zhēnguì zhī chù, jiù zàiyú tāmen zhǐ fāshēng yī cì, zhǐ bèi yī gè rén kàn dào, ránhòu yǒngyuǎn xiāoshī. Xiàng yī gè zhǐ duì nǐ yī gè rén jiǎng de mìmì.
text_en: Every morning I brew a cup of tea, then spend a few minutes watching the steam above the cup. Steam is transparent, but backlit you can see its shape—irregular wisps rising from the rim, twisting, rotating, spreading, eventually vanishing into the air. No two seconds of steam look the same. This may be the world's most fleeting artwork: to exist is to perish, to be born is to end. I've tried photographing steam, but the camera captures only a blurry white blur. Steam's beauty lies in its movement, and photographs are still. It's like trying to capture the beauty of music in a single photograph—you need the dimension of time. Some things are destined to be seen only by the present eye: unrecordable, unsaveable, unshareable. This isn't a regret—it's a privilege. Because it belongs only to the person looking at that exact moment. Our era treats 'shareability' as the standard of value—if you can't photograph it for social media, the experience almost doesn't exist. But steam reminds me that some of the best things are precisely unshareable. Their preciousness lies in happening only once, being seen by only one person, then vanishing forever. Like a secret told to no one but you.

questions.q_en: Why does the author say photographing steam is unsuccessful?

options.text: 蒸汽太小了，相机拍不清楚

options.text_en: Steam is too small for the camera to capture clearly

options.text: 光线条件不好
options.pinyin: guāngxiàn tiáojiàn bù hǎo
options.text_en: The lighting conditions were bad

questions.q_en: What is the author's attitude toward 'shareability' as a standard of value?

options.text: 他认为分享很重要

options.text_en: He thinks sharing is important

options.text: 他认为有些最好的东西恰恰是不可分享的

title: The Bench That Nobody Sits On
text_zh: 小区里有一张长椅，位置很好，在一棵大榕树下面，有树荫有微风，但几乎没有人坐。原因很简单：它旁边有一个垃圾桶。尽管环卫工人每天清理，垃圾桶并不臭，但人们还是本能地避开它。大家宁愿站着等人，也不愿坐在垃圾桶旁边。这种回避不是理性的判断，而是一种心理上的排斥。我偶尔会故意去坐那张长椅。坐下来之后发现，那其实是整个小区最安静的一个角落。因为没人来，也没人经过。垃圾桶反而变成了一道天然的屏障，把你和人群隔开。鸟在树上叫，风吹过榕树的气根，午后的阳光透过树叶落下来，形成一地斑驳的光影。这种体验是小区里其他长椅给不了的，因为那些长椅总是坐满了聊天的老人或者玩手机的年轻人。我开始想：生活中有多少好东西，是因为旁边放了一个「垃圾桶」而被我们错过的？一份工资不高但很有意义的工作、一个外表普通但内心丰富的人、一部评分不高但非常独特的电影。我们太习惯根据标签做判断了，以至于忘了亲自坐下来感受一下。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū lǐ yǒu yī zhāng chángyǐ, wèizhì hěn hǎo, zài yī kē dà róngshù xiàmian, yǒu shùyìn yǒu wēifēng, dàn jīhū méiyǒu rén zuò. Yuányīn hěn jiǎndān: tā pángbiān yǒu yī gè lājī tǒng. Jǐnguǎn huánwèi gōngrén měi tiān qīnglǐ, lājī tǒng bìng bú chòu, dàn rénmen háishi běnnéng de bìkāi tā. Dàjiā nìngyuàn zhàn zhe děng rén, yě bú yuàn zuò zài lājī tǒng pángbiān. Zhè zhǒng huíbì bú shì lǐxìng de pànduàn, ér shì yī zhǒng xīnlǐ shàng de páichì. Wǒ ǒu'ěr huì gùyì qù zuò nà zhāng chángyǐ. Zuò xiàlái zhīhòu fāxiàn, nà qíshí shì zhěnggè xiǎoqū zuì ānjìng de yī gè jiǎoluò. Yīnwèi méi rén lái, yě méi rén jīngguò. Lājī tǒng fǎn'ér biànchéng le yī dào tiānrán de píngzhàng, bǎ nǐ hé rénqún gé kāi. Niǎo zài shù shàng jiào, fēng chuī guò róngshù de qìgēn, wǔhòu de yángguāng tòu guò shùyè luò xiàlái, xíngchéng yī dì bānbó de guāngyǐng. Zhè zhǒng tǐyàn shì xiǎoqū lǐ qítā chángyǐ gěi bù liǎo de, yīnwèi nàxiē chángyǐ zǒng shì zuò mǎn le liáotiān de lǎorén huòzhě wán shǒujī de niánqīng rén. Wǒ kāishǐ xiǎng: shēnghuó zhōng yǒu duōshǎo hǎo dōngxi, shì yīnwèi pángbiān fàng le yī gè「lājī tǒng」ér bèi wǒmen cuòguò de? Yī fèn gōngzī bù gāo dàn hěn yǒu yìyì de gōngzuò, yī gè wàibiǎo pǔtōng dàn nèixīn fēngfù de rén, yī bù píngfēn bù gāo dàn fēicháng dútè de diànyǐng. Wǒmen tài xíguàn gēnjù biāoqiān zuò pànduàn le, yǐzhìyú wàng le qīnzì zuò xiàlái gǎnshòu yīxià.
text_en: In the residential compound there's a bench in an excellent spot—under a large banyan tree with shade and breeze—yet almost nobody sits on it. The reason is simple: there's a trash bin next to it. Though sanitation workers clean it daily and it doesn't smell, people instinctively avoid it. They'd rather stand and wait than sit beside a trash bin. This avoidance isn't rational judgment but psychological rejection. I occasionally sit there on purpose. Once seated, I discovered it's actually the quietest corner in the entire compound. Because nobody comes, nobody passes by. The trash bin has become a natural barrier separating you from the crowd. Birds sing in the tree, wind blows through the banyan's aerial roots, afternoon sunlight filters through leaves creating dappled patterns of light and shadow. This experience is something other benches in the compound can't offer, because those are always full of chatting elders or phone-scrolling young people. I began to wonder: how many good things in life do we miss because there's a 'trash bin' placed beside them? A low-paying but meaningful job, an ordinary-looking but richly inner person, a low-rated but genuinely unique film. We've grown too accustomed to judging by labels, forgetting to sit down and experience things for ourselves.

questions.q_en: Why does almost nobody sit on the well-positioned bench?

options.text_en: Because there's a trash bin next to it and people are psychologically repelled

options.text: 生活中那些让我们因为表面标签而错过好东西的负面因素

title: Two Ways of Folding Laundry
text_zh: 我跟妻子叠衣服的方式完全不同。她叠得很规整，每件T恤先对折袖子，再对折身体，最后再折一次，叠出来的大小一模一样，整整齐齐地码在衣柜里，像一家服装店的陈列。我呢，大概能分成「大致对折」和「揉成一团塞进去」两种模式，取决于我当时的心情和时间。我们为此争论过很多次。她觉得我叠的衣服会起皱，找的时候也不方便。我觉得反正穿在身上别人也看不出来是被精心叠过还是被随便塞过的。后来有一天，我无意中看到她叠完一摞衣服后，站在衣柜前看了好几秒。那个眼神不是在检查叠得整不整齐，而是在欣赏。那一刻我忽然明白了：叠衣服对她来说不只是一个家务动作，而是一种创造秩序的方式。她的生活可能在很多方面是混乱的——工作压力、孩子的事、跟父母的关系——但至少在这个衣柜面前，她能创造出一小片完美的秩序。叠衣服是她的冥想。从那以后我没有再嘲笑过她叠衣服。我甚至偶尔会认真地叠一次，然后叫她来看。她每次看了都会笑，那种笑不是因为我叠得好，而是因为我终于理解了她。
text_pinyin: Wǒ gēn qīzi dié yīfu de fāngshì wánquán bùtóng. Tā dié de hěn guīzhěng, měi jiàn T xù xiān duìzhé xiùzi, zài duìzhé shēntǐ, zuìhòu zài zhé yī cì, dié chūlái de dàxiǎo yīmóyīyàng, zhěngzhěng qíqí de mǎ zài yīguì lǐ, xiàng yī jiā fúzhuāng diàn de chénliè. Wǒ ne, dàgài néng fēnchéng「dàzhì duìzhé」hé「róu chéng yī tuán sāi jìnqù」liǎng zhǒng móshì, qǔjué yú wǒ dāngshí de xīnqíng hé shíjiān. Wǒmen wèi cǐ zhēnglùn guò hěn duō cì. Tā juéde wǒ dié de yīfu huì qǐ zhòu, zhǎo de shíhou yě bù fāngbiàn. Wǒ juéde fǎnzhèng chuān zài shēn shàng biéren yě kàn bù chūlái shì bèi jīngxīn dié guò háishi bèi suíbiàn sāi guò de. Hòulái yǒu yī tiān, wǒ wúyì zhōng kàn dào tā dié wán yī luò yīfu hòu, zhàn zài yīguì qián kàn le hǎo jǐ miǎo. Nàge yǎnshén bú shì zài jiǎnchá dié de zhěng bù zhěngqí, ér shì zài xīnshǎng. Nà yī kè wǒ hūrán míngbai le: dié yīfu duì tā lái shuō bù zhǐshì yī gè jiāwù dòngzuò, ér shì yī zhǒng chuàngzào zhìxù de fāngshì. Tā de shēnghuó kěnéng zài hěn duō fāngmiàn shì hǔnluàn de——gōngzuò yālì, háizi de shì, gēn fùmǔ de guānxi——dàn zhìshǎo zài zhège yīguì miànqián, tā néng chuàngzào chū yī xiǎo piàn wánměi de zhìxù. Dié yīfu shì tā de míngxiǎng. Cóng nà yǐhòu wǒ méiyǒu zài cháoxiào guò tā dié yīfu. Wǒ shènzhì ǒu'ěr huì rènzhēn de dié yī cì, ránhòu jiào tā lái kàn. Tā měi cì kàn le dōu huì xiào, nà zhǒng xiào bú shì yīnwèi wǒ dié de hǎo, ér shì yīnwèi wǒ zhōngyú lǐjiě le tā.
text_en: My wife and I fold laundry in completely different ways. She folds meticulously: each T-shirt gets its sleeves folded first, then the body folded, then folded once more—all coming out the same size, lined up neatly in the closet like a clothing store display. My approach roughly divides into 'approximate fold' and 'crumple into a ball and stuff it in,' depending on my mood and time. We've argued about this many times. She thinks my folding causes wrinkles and makes things hard to find. I figure nobody can tell whether what you're wearing was carefully folded or randomly stuffed. Then one day I happened to see her finish folding a stack and stand before the closet gazing at it for several seconds. Her expression wasn't checking neatness—she was admiring it. In that moment I suddenly understood: folding laundry for her isn't just a chore but a way of creating order. Her life may be chaotic in many ways—work stress, the children, her relationship with her parents—but at least before this closet, she can create a small patch of perfect order. Folding laundry is her meditation. After that I never made fun of her folding again. I even occasionally fold carefully and call her over to look. She always laughs when she sees it—not because I fold well, but because I've finally understood her.

questions.q_en: Why did the author stop making fun of his wife's folding?

options.text_en: Because he understood that folding was her way of creating order and inner peace

options.text: 因为他们达成了妥协

options.text_en: Because they reached a compromise

questions.q_zh: 妻子看到作者认真叠的衣服时为什么笑了？
questions.q_en: Why did the wife laugh when she saw the author's carefully folded laundry?

options.text: 因为他叠得还是很丑

options.text_en: Because his folding was still ugly

options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā zhōngyú lǐjiě le tā
options.text_en: Because he'd finally understood her

title: The Dogs Who Wait
text_zh: 每天早上九点左右，我家楼下的超市门口会出现三四只狗。它们的主人进去买东西，把它们拴在门口的柱子上或者自行车架上。这些狗彼此不认识，但因为共同的处境——被留下来等——它们形成了一种临时的群体。我观察过它们的行为，发现非常有意思。刚被拴住的头几分钟，每只狗都会盯着超市的大门，耳朵竖起来，随时准备迎接主人出来。大约五分钟后，焦虑开始出现。有的狗会小声呜咽，有的会原地转圈，有的会躺下来但眼睛始终盯着门口。再过五分钟，大部分狗会进入一种「接受」状态：它们不再盯着门口，开始观察周围的环境——经过的行人、路过的其他狗、地上的落叶。有一只黄色的中华田园犬特别有意思。它被拴住之后，先焦虑了两分钟，然后就完全放松了，趴在地上晒太阳，偶尔抬头看看四周，一脸平静。它似乎完全信任它的主人一定会回来，所以不浪费精力在焦虑上。我想，也许人类应该向这只狗学习。等待一个你信任的人，最好的方式不是焦虑地盯着门口，而是安心地趴下来晒太阳。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang jiǔ diǎn zuǒyòu, wǒ jiā lóu xià de chāoshì ménkǒu huì chūxiàn sān sì zhī gǒu. Tāmen de zhǔrén jìnqù mǎi dōngxi, bǎ tāmen shuān zài ménkǒu de zhùzi shàng huòzhě zìxíngchē jià shàng. Zhèxiē gǒu bǐcǐ bú rènshi, dàn yīnwèi gòngtóng de chǔjìng——bèi liú xiàlái děng——tāmen xíngchéng le yī zhǒng línshí de qúntǐ. Wǒ guānchá guò tāmen de xíngwéi, fāxiàn fēicháng yǒu yìsi. Gāng bèi shuān zhù de tóu jǐ fēnzhōng, měi zhī gǒu dōu huì dīng zhe chāoshì de dàmén, ěrduo shù qǐlái, suíshí zhǔnbèi yíngjiē zhǔrén chūlái. Dàyuē wǔ fēnzhōng hòu, jiāolǜ kāishǐ chūxiàn. Yǒu de gǒu huì xiǎo shēng wūyè, yǒu de huì yuándì zhuǎnquān, yǒu de huì tǎng xiàlái dàn yǎnjīng shǐzhōng dīng zhe ménkǒu. Zài guò wǔ fēnzhōng, dà bùfen gǒu huì jìnrù yī zhǒng「jiēshòu」zhuàngtài: tāmen bú zài dīng zhe ménkǒu, kāishǐ guānchá zhōuwéi de huánjìng——jīngguò de xíngrén, lùguò de qítā gǒu, dì shàng de luòyè. Yǒu yī zhī huángsè de zhōnghuá tiányuán quǎn tèbié yǒu yìsi. Tā bèi shuān zhù zhīhòu, xiān jiāolǜ le liǎng fēnzhōng, ránhòu jiù wánquán fàngsōng le, pā zài dì shàng shài tàiyáng, ǒu'ěr tái tóu kàn kàn sìzhōu, yī liǎn píngjìng. Tā sìhū wánquán xìnrèn tā de zhǔrén yīdìng huì huílái, suǒyǐ bù làngfèi jīnglì zài jiāolǜ shàng. Wǒ xiǎng, yěxǔ rénlèi yīnggāi xiàng zhè zhī gǒu xuéxí. Děngdài yī gè nǐ xìnrèn de rén, zuì hǎo de fāngshì bú shì jiāolǜ de dīng zhe ménkǒu, ér shì ānxīn de pā xiàlái shài tàiyáng.
text_en: Every morning around nine, three or four dogs appear outside the supermarket below my apartment. Their owners have gone in to shop, tying them to the doorway pillars or bicycle racks. These dogs don't know each other, but their shared situation—being left behind to wait—forms them into a temporary group. I've observed their behavior and found it fascinating. During the first few minutes of being tied up, every dog stares at the supermarket door, ears pricked, ready to greet their returning owner. About five minutes later, anxiety sets in. Some whimper softly, some pace in circles, some lie down but keep their eyes fixed on the entrance. After another five minutes, most dogs enter an 'acceptance' state: they stop watching the door and begin observing their surroundings—passing pedestrians, other dogs, leaves on the ground. One yellow Chinese field dog was especially interesting. After being tied up, it was anxious for about two minutes, then completely relaxed—lying on the ground sunbathing, occasionally lifting its head to look around, perfectly calm. It seemed to completely trust that its owner would return, so it wasted no energy on anxiety. I think perhaps humans should learn from this dog. The best way to wait for someone you trust isn't anxiously watching the door, but peacefully lying down in the sun.

questions.q_zh: 狗被拴住后大约经历了哪几个阶段？
questions.q_en: What stages do dogs go through after being tied up?

options.text: 兴奋→睡觉→焦虑
options.pinyin: xīngfèn → shuìjiào → jiāolǜ
options.text_en: Excitement → sleeping → anxiety

options.text: 盯着门口→焦虑→接受并观察周围
options.pinyin: dīng zhe ménkǒu → jiāolǜ → jiēshòu bìng guānchá zhōuwéi
options.text_en: Watching the door → anxiety → acceptance and observing surroundings

options.text: 安静→叫→继续安静
options.pinyin: ānjìng → jiào → jìxù ānjìng
options.text_en: Quiet → barking → quiet again

questions.q_zh: 作者认为那只黄色的狗能迅速放松的原因是什么？
questions.q_en: Why does the author think the yellow dog could relax so quickly?

options.text: 它性格本来就很懒
options.pinyin: tā xìnggé běnlái jiù hěn lǎn
options.text_en: It was naturally lazy

options.text: 它完全信任主人一定会回来
options.pinyin: tā wánquán xìnrèn zhǔrén yīdìng huì huílái
options.text_en: It completely trusted its owner would return

options.text: 它已经习惯被拴在外面了
options.pinyin: tā yǐjīng xíguàn bèi shuān zài wàimiàn le
options.text_en: It was used to being tied up outside

title: The Way Light Enters a Room
text_zh: 搬进新家之后，我花了大约一个月的时间才真正了解光线进入这个房间的方式。早上七点，太阳从东边窗户进来，先照到书架的上半部分，然后慢慢移到桌面上。到了九点，整个书桌都被阳光覆盖，这是一天中最适合阅读的时刻，因为光线充足但不刺眼。中午的时候，太阳绕到了南面，从阳台的落地窗射进来，在客厅地板上形成一个巨大的长方形光斑。这个光斑会随着太阳的移动缓缓滑过地板，像一把看不见的扫帚在慢慢扫地。下午三四点，光线开始变得柔和，从西边的小窗户进来，带着一种温暖的琥珀色调，这时候适合坐在沙发上发呆。到了五点半，最后一缕阳光从西窗的上沿斜射进来，刚好照在墙上的一幅画上，持续大约十分钟。这幅画是我在跳蚤市场买的一幅旧油画，平时看起来颜色有些暗淡，但在那十分钟里，它好像被重新点亮了。我没有刻意安排这一切。房子的朝向、窗户的位置、家具的摆放，这些因素碰巧组合在一起，创造了一个关于光的叙事。每天从日出到日落，光线在这个房间里讲述着一个完整的故事。而我，只是恰好住在这个故事里。
text_pinyin: Bān jìn xīn jiā zhīhòu, wǒ huā le dàyuē yī gè yuè de shíjiān cái zhēnzhèng liǎojiě guāngxiàn jìnrù zhège fángjiān de fāngshì. Zǎoshang qī diǎn, tàiyáng cóng dōngbiān chuānghu jìnlái, xiān zhào dào shūjià de shàng bàn bùfen, ránhòu mànmàn yí dào zhuōmiàn shàng. Dào le jiǔ diǎn, zhěnggè shūzhuō dōu bèi yángguāng fùgài, zhè shì yī tiān zhōng zuì shìhé yuèdú de shíkè, yīnwèi guāngxiàn chōngzú dàn bú cìyǎn. Zhōngwǔ de shíhou, tàiyáng rào dào le nánmiàn, cóng yángtái de luòdì chuāng shè jìnlái, zài kètīng dìbǎn shàng xíngchéng yī gè jùdà de chángfāngxíng guāng bān. Zhège guāng bān huì suí zhe tàiyáng de yídòng huǎnhuǎn huá guò dìbǎn, xiàng yī bǎ kàn bú jiàn de sǎozhou zài mànmàn sǎo dì. Xiàwǔ sān sì diǎn, guāngxiàn kāishǐ biàn de róuhé, cóng xībiān de xiǎo chuānghu jìnlái, dài zhe yī zhǒng wēnnuǎn de hǔpò sèdiào, zhè shíhou shìhé zuò zài shāfā shàng fādāi. Dào le wǔ diǎn bàn, zuìhòu yī lǚ yángguāng cóng xī chuāng de shàng yán xiéshè jìnlái, gānghǎo zhào zài qiáng shàng de yī fú huà shàng, chíxù dàyuē shí fēnzhōng. Zhè fú huà shì wǒ zài tiàozǎo shìchǎng mǎi de yī fú jiù yóuhuà, píngshí kàn qǐlái yánsè yǒuxiē àndàn, dàn zài nà shí fēnzhōng lǐ, tā hǎoxiàng bèi chóngxīn diǎn liàng le. Wǒ méiyǒu kèyì ānpái zhè yīqiè. Fángzi de cháoxiàng, chuānghu de wèizhì, jiājù de bǎifàng, zhèxiē yīnsù qiǎohǎo zǔhé zài yīqǐ, chuàngzào le yī gè guānyú guāng de xùshì. Měi tiān cóng rì chū dào rìluò, guāngxiàn zài zhège fángjiān lǐ jiǎngshù zhe yī gè wánzhěng de gùshì. Ér wǒ, zhǐshì qiǎohǎo zhù zài zhège gùshì lǐ.
text_en: After moving into my new home, it took about a month to truly understand how light enters this room. At seven in the morning, the sun comes through the east window, first hitting the upper half of the bookshelf, then slowly moving to the desk. By nine, the entire desk is bathed in sunlight—the best reading moment of the day, with ample but non-glaring light. At noon, the sun has moved to the south, pouring through the balcony's floor-to-ceiling window, forming a huge rectangular patch of light on the living room floor. This patch glides slowly across the floor as the sun moves, like an invisible broom sweeping. Around three or four in the afternoon, the light softens, entering through a small west-side window with a warm amber tone—perfect for sitting on the sofa and daydreaming. At five-thirty, the last slant of sunlight enters from the upper edge of the west window, landing precisely on a painting on the wall for about ten minutes. It's an old oil painting I bought at a flea market—normally its colors seem somewhat muted, but during those ten minutes it seems to be reilluminated. I didn't deliberately arrange any of this. The building's orientation, the windows' positions, the furniture placement—these factors happened to combine and create a narrative about light. Every day from sunrise to sunset, light tells a complete story in this room. And I simply happen to live inside that story.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为一天中最适合阅读的时刻是什么时候？
questions.q_en: When does the author consider the best reading moment of the day?

options.text: 早上九点左右，光线充足但不刺眼
options.pinyin: zǎoshang jiǔ diǎn zuǒyòu, guāngxiàn chōngzú dàn bú cìyǎn
options.text_en: Around 9 a.m., when light is ample but not glaring

options.text: 下午三四点，光线柔和
options.pinyin: xiàwǔ sān sì diǎn, guāngxiàn róuhé
options.text_en: 3-4 p.m. when light is soft

options.text: 中午，阳光最强的时候
options.pinyin: zhōngwǔ, yángguāng zuì qiáng de shíhou
options.text_en: Noon, when sunlight is strongest

questions.q_zh: 文章最后说「我只是恰好住在这个故事里」，这表达了什么感受？
questions.q_en: What does the final sentence 'I simply happen to live inside that story' express?

options.text: 他对新家的光线设计很满意
options.pinyin: tā duì xīn jiā de guāngxiàn shèjì hěn mǎnyì
options.text_en: He's satisfied with his new home's lighting design

options.text: 一种对日常之美的偶然性和感恩
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng duì rìcháng zhī měi de ǒurán xìng hé gǎn'ēn
options.text_en: A sense of the serendipity and gratitude for everyday beauty

options.text: 他觉得自己只是房间的一部分
options.pinyin: tā juéde zìjǐ zhǐshì fángjiān de yī bùfen
options.text_en: He feels he is just part of the room
